title: The Incarnation Is a Model for Generosity, Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Incarnation is a Model for Generosity_ Part Two (Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: 2 Corinthians 8:8-12: the test of love through giving
Not a command but proof of genuine love (v.8)
For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ (v.9)
Proportional giving: give according to what you have (vv.11-12)
The willing mind is accepted based on what one has, not what one does not have
summary: Second part of the sermon on Christ’s incarnation as a model for generosity. Continuing in 2 Corinthians 8:8-12, Paul presents giving not as a command but as a test and proof of genuine love—motivated by the grace of Christ who became poor for our sake. Emphasizes that God accepts proportional giving based on what one has, not what one lacks, and that the willing mind is what matters.
transcript:
## Why Christians Give

We are going to continue talking about Christian generosity, because generosity is a virtue that must be present in our lives. It points us back to the generosity of God — a generous God, the God of the Bible who has given us all things. It also points us back to the ultimate gift: the Lord Jesus Christ, as Paul concludes this section of 2 Corinthians in 9:15 with thanking God for his indescribable gift.

This afternoon's focus is on financial giving. I want to answer the question: how does the idea of generosity apply to financial giving? I'll give three reasons why Christians should give, and then two answers to what Christians should give.

### Reason 1: Giving Shows How Great God Is

The man who gladly sold all he had to obtain the pearl of great price demonstrates this. The Bible's logic concerning generosity is simple: give up what is valuable to gain what is more valuable. When we give, we show that God is worth more than anything we possess.

### Reason 2: God Generously Gives to Us

> *"For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though He was rich, yet for your sake He became poor, that you through His poverty might become rich."* (2 Corinthians 8:9)

We give because doing so acknowledges the spiritual abundance we have been given through the gospel and brings glory to God. As Paul says in Romans 8:32, God gave us his most precious gift — his Son — and because of that, he will not deny us anything.

### Reason 3: Giving Promotes Godliness

> *"Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also."* (Matthew 6:21)

We cannot trust in God and in our possessions simultaneously. When we give, we recognize that everything already belongs to God. That is why in Malachi 3, God accuses Israel of robbing him for not tithing — even what they kept already belonged to him. Giving also encourages others to give thanks and praise God (2 Corinthians 9:12), and brings genuine blessing:

> *"He who sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and he who sows bountifully will also reap bountifully."* (2 Corinthians 9:6)

> *"Give, and it will be given to you. Good measure, pressed down, shaken together and running over, will be put into your bosom."* (Luke 6:38)

Refusing God's reward is not piety — it defames God in his delight in showing mercy.

## What Christians Should Give

### Everything

The first answer is: everything. Our calling is not simply to live on as little as possible so we can give the most to the local church. Rather, the question is: how is every penny bringing glory to God? Whether you spend money fixing your plumbing — freeing time to serve a discouraged friend — or give it directly to ministry, you are using God's resources for his glory. As Paul writes:

> *"Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God."* (1 Corinthians 10:31)

The church should not hoard large savings with no purpose; that money should be actively invested for the kingdom. The same applies to our retirement thinking — every penny should be working for the Lord.

### Tithes and Offerings

The second answer is tithes and offerings. Those who argue against tithing rarely do so because they want to give fifty percent. In the Old Testament, Israel observed three distinct tithes: a tithe for the Levites (Leviticus 27:30–33), a tithe for the annual feast at the central sanctuary (Deuteronomy 12:17–18), and a triennial tithe for the poor, foreigners, orphans, and widows. Added together with the temple tax and voluntary offerings, the Old Testament system of giving exceeded thirty percent.

But note that tithing predates the Mosaic law — Abraham gave a tenth to Melchizedek, and Jacob promised a tenth to God, hundreds of years before Sinai. The tithe transcends the Mosaic administration of the covenant of grace.

In the New Testament, we are never prohibited from giving ten percent. Paul teaches proportional, indexed giving:

> *"On the first day of the week let each of you lay something aside, storing up as he may prosper."* (1 Corinthians 16:2)

Paul also teaches sacrificial giving. Jesus makes self-denial non-negotiable for discipleship:

> *"If anyone desires to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me."* (Matthew 16:24)

## Two Errors to Avoid

**The first error** is thinking that if your budget is not painfully tight, you are not giving enough. It is not wrong to accumulate wealth — Abraham, Job, and Jacob all did so with God's blessing. But regardless of income level, every Christian should practice uncomfortable, sacrificial giving in some area: money, time, relationships, or skills.

**The second error** is believing that comfort and Christianity can coexist. Paul writes:

> *"You therefore must endure hardship as a good soldier of Jesus Christ. No one engaged in warfare entangles himself with the affairs of this life."* (2 Timothy 2:3–4)

The Christian life is compared to a soldier and a farmer — neither is a picture of ease. This does not mean you cannot have a comfortable chair or car. It means you cannot be satisfied with living only for yourself.

## Conclusion

How much should Christians give? Everything. And of what you retain, be able to say honestly before God that the money kept back brings him equal or greater glory than if it were given away. God calls us to be generous because he is generous — and as Jesus became poor for our sakes, we follow him as generous people.
